rationale: Hospital pressure is visible, urgent and fundable; upstream failure is dispersed.
Hospital operational risk may dominate internal allocation attention.
Weak marginal contact payment can constrain additional clinically necessary work.
Consumers route around delays via co-payment, telehealth, ambulance or ED.
PHO functions may add value, but payment intermediation may add friction.
ACC activity funding may stabilise supply; isolating it may shift pressure elsewhere.
Ambulance alternatives need funding, governance and follow-up.
Funding eligibility may be narrower than safe clinical scope.
Telehealth extends access but may erode local supply if poorly integrated.
Co-payment can signal demand or deter necessary care.
Top-tier KPIs determine what becomes managed.
Access benefits do not replace relational and kaupapa Maori/Pacific/locality functions.
Reform framing determines coalition formation and resistance.
Unobserved upstream need remains less fundable than visible hospital pressure.
